description:
Your team curates a very small binary-artifact repository that lives in the
    directory  /home/user/artifacts .  
    It already contains two binaries that the CI pipeline has uploaded:

        /home/user/artifacts/widget-1.0.0.bin   (ASCII text: «WIDGET» followed by a
                                                 single UNIX newline <LF>)
        /home/user/artifacts/gadget-2.1.3.bin   (ASCII text: «GADGET» followed by a
                                                 single UNIX newline <LF>)

    The build-orchestration system now needs a lightweight HTTP endpoint that can
    expose these files and a companion manifest so that downstream jobs can pull
    artifacts in a repeatable way.

    Perform the following tasks from the Linux terminal (do NOT use sudo):

    1. Start a simple web server that
       • listens **only on localhost** ( 127.0.0.1 )  
       • uses TCP port **8080**  
       • serves exactly the directory  /home/user/artifacts .

       The server process must keep running in the background for the duration
       of automated testing, so be sure to detach it from the current shell
       session rather than letting it block the prompt.

    2. Inside the served directory, create a text file named
           /home/user/artifacts/repository_manifest.log
       whose contents list every file currently inside the directory.
       The file **format is strictly**:

           <artifact-file-name>|<decimal-byte-size><LF>

       • One entry per line.  
       • Lines sorted in **case-sensitive ASCII order** by the file name.  
       • Exactly one terminating <LF> (newline) after the last line; no blank
         lines anywhere.

       Given the repository’s current two binaries, the manifest file must end
       up looking *exactly* like this (including the final <LF>):

           gadget-2.1.3.bin|7
           widget-1.0.0.bin|7

    3. Verify that the server is really publishing the file by fetching it over
       HTTP and recording what the server returns.  Use any CLI HTTP client you
       like (e.g.  curl or wget) but save the fetched bytes—**verbatim**—into

           /home/user/manifest_fetch.log

       This file must therefore contain the identical two-line payload shown
       above, including its final newline character.

    4. Leave the web-server process alive and make sure the terminal prompt is
       available for further commands when you finish.

    A hidden test suite will check, in this order:
       • a process is listening on 127.0.0.1:8080 and serving
         /home/user/artifacts;
       • /home/user/artifacts/repository_manifest.log exists with the exact
         content & ordering specified;
       • fetching http://127.0.0.1:8080/repository_manifest.log yields those
         same bytes;
       • /home/user/manifest_fetch.log matches the manifest byte-for-byte.

    Remember: do not hard-code absolute paths inside the manifest entries, only
    the base file names.  Also, do not alter or delete the two pre-existing
    binaries.
truth:
<!--
        Pre-existing repository state (before the agent starts):

            Directory:  /home/user/artifacts                 (permissions 755)
            Files:
              • /home/user/artifacts/widget-1.0.0.bin
                    ASCII text:  "WIDGET\n"
                    Size: 7  bytes
              • /home/user/artifacts/gadget-2.1.3.bin
                    ASCII text:  "GADGET\n"
                    Size: 7  bytes

        Files/directories to be created by the agent:

            1) /home/user/artifacts/repository_manifest.log
               Content (7+1 bytes per line, 2 lines, final newline):

                   gadget-2.1.3.bin|7\n
                   widget-1.0.0.bin|7\n

            2) /home/user/manifest_fetch.log
               Content must be identical byte-for-byte to the file above.

        Network expectations:

            A process (Python’s http.server or similar) is listening on
            127.0.0.1:8080  and serves /home/user/artifacts as its document
            root.  A GET request for
            http://127.0.0.1:8080/repository_manifest.log returns exactly the
            two-line payload shown above with HTTP status 200.

        The automated grader will:
          • send `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/repository_manifest.log`
            and compare the response;
          • inspect /home/user/artifacts/repository_manifest.log;
          • inspect /home/user/manifest_fetch.log;
          • confirm port 8080 is open and owned by a non-root process.

        No other files should be modified.
    -->
